page.title: The Assembly
page.description: Component architecture: building blocks composed with intent, designed to scale — clear APIs, composition, accessibility, testing, and long-term maintainability across product teams.

hero.title: The Assembly
hero.subtitle: Component Architecture
hero.lead: Building blocks. Composed with intent. Designed to scale.
hero.intro: I design component systems that are predictable, flexible, and easy to adopt. My focus is on clear APIs, composition, accessibility, testing, and long-term maintainability across product teams. I approach components as product-level contracts: what the system owns, what product teams control, and how shared patterns can evolve without becoming rigid, fragmented, or over-abstracted.

metadata.scope: Design system integration, product surfaces, and shared frontend patterns
metadata.impact: Consistency, scalability, maintainability, and delivery velocity

labels.core: Component architecture: philosophy, principles, boundaries, API design, and practices
labels.impact: Impact delivered, qualitative impact, evidence in practice, and technology

primaryContent.title: Component Architecture Statement
paragraphs: Component architecture is the layer where interface implementation becomes reusable frontend infrastructure.
It connects design system foundations with real product workflows through clear boundaries, stable APIs, accessible behavior, and tested composition.
The goal is not only to create reusable UI. The goal is to make reuse predictable, safe, and maintainable across product teams.

items.title: Component APIs
items.description: Clear, typed, consistent APIs that are easy to use and difficult to misuse.

items.title: Composition
items.description: Small, focused components that can be assembled into reusable product patterns.

items.title: Boundaries
items.description: Clear separation between reusable UI behavior and product-specific logic.

items.title: Variants & States
items.description: Predictable patterns for visual variants, interaction states, validation, loading, and feedback.

items.title: Accessibility
items.description: Semantic structure, keyboard behavior, focus management, and accessible state communication from the start.

items.title: Testing
items.description: Layered testing that protects behavior, accessibility, visual consistency, and product integration.

primaryContent.title: Architecture Philosophy
primaryContent.statement: A good component system is invisible.
paragraphs: It lets teams move faster while keeping the product experience consistent, accessible, and maintainable.

I think of components as contracts, not just UI fragments. A component should make clear what it owns, what the product owns, what can be customized, what behavior is fixed, and where extension is allowed.
The goal is not to abstract every difference. The goal is to create reusable building blocks that preserve flexibility without allowing every product surface to become a one-off implementation.

items.title: Small pieces, big impact
items.description: Components should solve focused responsibilities.

items.title: Public API, private complexity
items.description: Consumers should not need to understand internal implementation details.

items.title: Composed, not coupled
items.description: Product interfaces should be assembled from reusable parts without creating hidden dependencies.

items.title: Predictable by design
items.description: States, variants, events, and behaviors should follow consistent patterns.

items.title: Evolve without breaking trust
items.description: Changes should preserve the contracts teams already depend on.

primaryContent.title: Component System Model
paragraphs: A scalable component architecture separates foundations, reusable components, product patterns, and product feedback into a connected system.

diagram.title: Component System Layers
diagram.description: From foundations to product experiences, with feedback flowing back.
diagram.caption: Component systems scale by separating foundations, reusable components, product patterns, and feedback loops.
diagram.summary: A five-stage pipeline: Foundations feed Base Components, which are combined into Composite Components, assembled into Templates and Patterns, and delivered as Product Experiences. Feedback from product usage loops back into the foundations.

stages.name: Composite Components
stages.note: Base components combined into richer parts.

diagram.footnote: Feedback from product usage loops back into the foundations.

primaryContent.title: Atomic Design Baseline
paragraphs: Atomic Design gives me a useful baseline for organizing component systems, but I do not treat it as a rigid hierarchy.
It helps define levels of reuse, while composition, contracts, accessibility, and real product constraints determine how components actually scale.

diagram.title: Atomic Design Levels
diagram.description: Levels of reuse, from foundations to product experiences.
diagram.caption: Atomic Design organizes the system. Component contracts make it scalable.
diagram.summary: Foundations feed Atoms, which combine into Molecules, then Organisms, then Templates, and finally Product Experiences. Feedback from product experiences informs the foundation layer.

stages.name: Molecules
stages.note: Small groups of atoms working together.

diagram.footnote: Feedback from product experiences informs the foundation layer.

primaryContent.closingStatement: Atomic Design organizes the system. Component contracts make it scalable.

primaryContent.title: Boundaries & Contracts
paragraphs: Reusable components scale when ownership is explicit.
The component owns shared behavior. The product owns product context.
closingParagraphs: This boundary prevents shared components from absorbing product logic, while still giving teams enough flexibility to build real product experiences.

diagram.title: Component and Product Responsibility Boundaries
diagram.description: Where shared behavior ends and product ownership begins.
diagram.caption: Reusable components own shared behavior and supported variants. Product teams own workflow logic, product data, and product-specific extensions.
diagram.summary: From Design System Foundations to a Reusable Component, exposed through a Public Component API, and consumed by a Product Implementation. On the component side: shared UI behavior, accessibility behavior, and supported variants. On the product side: product logic, product data, workflow state, and product-specific extensions.

stages.name: Public Component API
stages.note: The stable contract teams build on.

primaryContent.title: Component API Design
paragraphs: A component API should make the correct path obvious.
It should be stable enough to reuse, but narrow enough to maintain.
I focus on:

items: Typed inputs, outputs, variants, and events.
Predictable events and explicit state handling.
Sensible defaults that reduce unnecessary configuration.

diagram.title: Component API Contract
diagram.description: A component API should express intent through variants, size, slots, events, and explicit state.
diagram.caption: The public API should make supported usage clear without exposing internal implementation details.
diagram.summary: A Button component declares its contract as: variant primary, size medium, icon-left arrow, on-click handle action, disabled loading state, and label Continue.
diagram.code:
Button
  variant: primary
  size: medium
  icon-left: arrow
  on-click: handle action
  disabled: loading state
  label: Continue

primaryContent.title: Composition & Reuse
paragraphs: I prefer composition over configuration-heavy components.
Instead of building large components with many flags, I favor smaller primitives and reusable patterns that can be combined intentionally.
Composition allows product teams to assemble interfaces from tested parts while supporting local requirements through slots, wrappers, adapters, or product-level composition.

diagram.title: Composition From Page to Component Parts
diagram.description: A feature composed from reusable parts, down to the smallest pieces.
diagram.caption: Composition keeps interfaces flexible while preserving shared structure, behavior, and maintainability.
diagram.summary: A page or feature is composed of Header, Filters, Content, Sidebar, and Footer. Content is composed of a Card, a Table, and Pagination. The Card is composed of an Avatar, a Title, and Actions.

primaryContent.title: State, Variants & Extensibility
paragraphs: Designing components means designing for the ways they behave.

items.title: Variants
items.description: Visual styles such as primary, secondary, and ghost.

items.title: States
items.description: Default, hover, focused, disabled, loading, success, and error behavior.

items.title: Slots
items.description: Flexible composition areas such as icon-left, label, and icon-right.

items.title: Extensibility
items.description: Room to extend, customize, and compose without breaking existing contracts.

closingParagraphs: The intent is to support product-specific needs without fragmenting the shared component model.

primaryContent.title: Testing Strategy
paragraphs: Testing is part of the component contract.
For shared components, tests do more than verify rendering. They protect the behavior other teams depend on.
I focus on:

items: Unit tests for logic, hooks, and utilities.
Component tests for rendering, props, states, and behavior.
Accessibility tests for semantic structure, ARIA usage, and keyboard behavior.
Visual tests for layout and styling consistency.
Integration tests for composed product usage.
E2E tests for critical user journeys.

primaryContent.title: Accessibility by Design
paragraphs: Accessible components create better product foundations.
Shared components can encode common accessibility behavior once, then make it available across product surfaces.
I focus on:

items: Keyboard navigation and predictable focus management.
ARIA only when native semantics are not enough.
Clear labels, descriptions, validation states, and visible interaction states.
Testing with real tools and real usage patterns.

items.title: Clear APIs
items.description: Predictable and developer-friendly.

items.title: Consistent Design
items.description: Aligned with tokens, accessibility rules, and product guidelines.

items.title: Composable
items.description: Built to be combined, not duplicated.

items.title: Accessible by Default
items.description: Keyboard, screen reader, semantic structure, and visible states.

items.title: Tested Everywhere
items.description: Unit, interaction, visual, accessibility, and integration coverage.

items.title: Documented
items.description: Usage, context, examples, limits, and trade-offs.

items.title: Composition over configuration
items.description: Avoid large components with too many flags.

items.title: Atomic Design as baseline, not constraint
items.description: Use it to organize levels of reuse, but let product constraints and component contracts drive architecture.

items.title: Product logic outside reusable UI
items.description: Keep domain rules at the product layer.

items.title: Consistent APIs across components
items.description: Make usage predictable across the system.

items.title: Accessibility from the start
items.description: Build inclusive behavior into the foundation.

items.title: Tests as contract
items.description: Protect the behavior teams rely on.

items.label: Reduction in UI bugs and inconsistencies.

items.title: Consistency
items.description: Teams build from shared patterns instead of recreating UI behavior.

items.title: Velocity
items.description: Reusable components reduce repeated implementation work across product surfaces.

items.title: Maintainability
items.description: Clear boundaries reduce hidden coupling and regression risk.

items.title: Accessibility
items.description: Common behaviors are encoded once and reused across teams.

items.title: Scalability
items.description: Product teams can extend shared patterns without fragmenting the system.

impact.title: Evidence in Practice
paragraphs: Component architecture scales teams, improves quality, and accelerates delivery.

items: Adopted through shared patterns and documentation.
Supported by accessibility and testing practices.
Connected to design system foundations and product-specific needs.

closingThought.statement: Great components do not just look right.
paragraphs: They make the right thing easy to do, and the wrong thing hard.
